text: The mission of Jesus to Judea in John 3:22–36 has been an infrequently explored topic within Jesus studies. This relative neglect is closely tied to the identification of the “Jew” who complains to Caiaphas about the dispute between John’s disciples and the Jew’s own disciples in 3:25. This article will seek to shed new light on the geography of Jesus’ mission within John 2:13–4:54 and examine the implications for this identification. The geography of Jesus’ mission in this section of the Gospel of John will be examined closely, and it will be argued that Jesus’ mission within this section is structured according to a geography described in Acts 1:8 (see also 8:25; 26; 39) that stretches from Jerusalem and Judea to Samaria and to the “ends of the earth.” If this structure is correct, then it continues on into Galilee, so that by the time of Jesus’ conflicts with the Judean Jewish leaders in chapters 5–10, all of God’s people in Israel have heard and believed Jesus’ message. A new identification of the “Jew” of 3:25 could then also be proposed, which understands the term Ἰουδαῖος to refer to a Judean (as in Judea) rather than to one of the broader identity group of Jews.
headline: Fully Human Written
